topic: Someone finds their college application essay published as an example
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp, cream-colored paper felt alien in my hands. It was my essay, the one I’d poured weeks of agonizing introspection into—a rambling, deeply personal narrative about navigating grief through obscure poetry. But the typeface was wrong. The header read, in bold, elegant script: "Sample Application Essay: Finding Your Voice." Beneath it, a placeholder name, "Jane Doe," was printed in a mocking, standardized font. A cold knot tightened in my stomach, spreading outward like ice water. I flipped through the pages, the familiar paragraphs mocking me from the page numbers. My jaw clenched so hard I thought my teeth might crack. Someone had taken my vulnerability, my very essence, and reduced it to a template for someone else’s aspirations. I crumpled the paper slowly, deliberately, the sound of the tearing sound loud in the quiet dorm room.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen, my breath catching in my throat. It was on the university admissions portal, a section meant for viewing successful submissions. And there it was: my story. Every carefully chosen metaphor, every hesitant sentence, laid bare for casual inspection. A flush crept up my neck, hot and furious. I slammed my laptop shut, the plastic protesting the sudden violence. My hands were trembling, a shaky betrayal of the rage bubbling beneath my composure. "This isn't fair," I muttered, the words tasting like ash. I stood up, pacing the small room, running a hand through my hair until it was a tangled mess. I needed to find the person who had done this. I needed to know who had decided my private struggle was fodder for their public display.

[story 3]

The fluorescent lights of the library seemed to hum with malicious intent. I found the file tucked away in a shared drive, a ghost in the machine. My heart began to hammer against my ribs, a frantic, uneven rhythm. When I finally opened the document, the realization hit me with physical force. It was mine. My confession, my journey, formatted perfectly, sitting beside other hopeful, generic narratives. A deep, burning sensation started in my chest, a pressure building behind my eyes. I leaned back in my chair, the leather squeaking under my weight, and let out a sharp, humorless laugh. It was a sound devoid of joy, purely sharp annoyance. I picked up a pen, not to write, but to aggressively underline the words "navigating grief," drawing harsh, jagged lines across the perfectly crafted prose.
